description: Figure 1 presents a series of eight optical micrographs showing the as-cast microstructures of Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C sheet castings with increasing Mn contents: (a) 5, (b) 10, (c) 15, (d) 20, (e) 25, (f) 30, (g) 35, and (h) 40 mass% Mn. All images share a 100 μm scale bar and reveal dendritic solidification morphologies. Macroscopically, the microstructures consist of a duplex mixture of white α phase (ordered D03 ferrite) and darker γ phase (austenite). As Mn content increases from 5 to 40 mass%, the α phase fraction progressively decreases from a majority constituent to near elimination, while the γ phase becomes increasingly dominant. At 40 mass% Mn, the alloy is essentially single-phase γ with only trace residual α. The micrographs visually confirm that Mn strongly stabilizes austenite in this alloy system and support the reported quantitative trend that α phase fraction decreases with increasing Mn content following an empirical quadratic relationship.

This bright-field transmission electron micrograph (Fig. 2) shows the as-cast Fe–10Al–15Mn–1.0C alloy (alloy C) with a scale bar of 4.0 μm. The image reveals a duplex microstructure consisting of two distinct regions: region A, identified as the α-ferrite phase with an ordered D03 structure, and region B, identified as the γ-austenite phase. Coarse κ'' precipitates, labeled κ'' and identified by the caption and text as (Fe,Mn)3AlCx carbides having an ordered L12-type structure, are visible within region A. The micrograph visually demonstrates that these κ'' carbides preferentially precipitate along the γ/α phase boundaries at a higher density than within the D03 matrix interior. This figure supports the key conclusion that the macroscopic microstructure of the 15Mn alloy is a mixture of (γ + fine κ + κ') and (D03 + coarse κ'') regions, and that the κ'' carbides exhibit preferential boundary segregation rather than uniform matrix distribution.

Figure 4 presents a selected area electron diffraction pattern (SAED) taken from a coarse particle in the as-cast Fe-10Al-xMn-1.0C alloy. The image displays an array of diffraction spots indexed with Miller indices including 000, 010, 020, 100, 110, 120, 200, 210, and 220. The caption identifies the source as the coarse particle marked as kappa-prime-prime in Fig. 2. The accompanying text reports that this particle is a (Fe,Mn)3AlCx carbide exhibiting an ordered L12-type structure, as deduced from the superlattice spots 100, 110, and 120 visible in the pattern. The lattice parameter is stated to be a = 0.387–0.389 nm. This diffraction evidence supports the conclusion that the carbide possesses an ordered L12 structure and likely formed at elevated temperature with a more disordered carbon distribution compared to finer kappa and kappa-prime precipitates in the austenite matrix.

Figure 3 presents a TEM investigation of the austenite (γ) region in as-cast Fe–10Al–15Mn–1.0C alloy (alloy C). (a) A bright-field micrograph from region B shows coarser precipitates (C) and extremely fine precipitates (D) within the γ matrix. (b) and (c) are [001] selected-area diffraction patterns from regions C and D, respectively; both display fundamental γ reflections and weak superlattice spots (e.g., 100, 110, 010). The markedly stronger 100 superlattice intensity relative to 110 indicates an ordered L′1₂-type structure for the (Fe,Mn)₃AlCₓ carbides (designated κ′ and κ). Elongated satellites visible around the (200) and (220) reflections are consistent with spinodal decomposition or chemical fluctuation during cooling. (d) A dark-field image taken with the 100 κ′ reflection maps the fine κ′ carbide distribution and confirms that κ′ and κ share the same orientation. Together, these micrographs and diffraction patterns demonstrate that ordered nano-scale κ′/κ carbides precipitate in the γ matrix, supporting the proposed strengthening mechanism in this Fe–Al–Mn–C alloy system.

Figure 7 presents transmission electron microscopy characterization of the ordered D03 α-ferrite phase in as-cast Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C alloy sheet castings. The figure comprises two selected-area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns and two corresponding dark-field electron micrographs. The SAED patterns display D03 superlattice reflections including 200, 220, 020, 111 and 022 spots, confirming the ordered D03 crystal structure of the α phase. Panel (a) is a g = 111 dark-field image and panel (b) is a g = 200 dark-field image, both sharing the same magnification as indicated by the 0.5 μm scale bar. The dark-field micrographs reveal a network of anti-phase boundaries (APBs) and ordered domain configurations within the D03 matrix, characterized by irregular, interconnected bright and dark contrast regions. These images provide direct diffraction and real-space evidence that the α phase adopts the D03 ordered structure and contains APBs, supporting the paper’s microstructural model that the macroscopic α region consists of a D03 matrix with coarse κ'' carbide precipitates.

Figure 6 shows a selected area diffraction pattern (SADP) taken from the D03 matrix and the κ'' phase particle in an as-cast Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C alloy sheet. The pattern visually superimposes two reciprocal lattices: a dash-dot hexagon marks the D03 matrix reflections (including 000, 200, 220, and 400 spots), while a solid-line hexagon marks the κ phase reflections (indexed as 111, 111̄, 200, etc.). The caption identifies these as the D03 matrix and κ phase, respectively. According to the citation text, this SADP establishes the orientation relationship between the ordered D03 ferrite matrix (lattice parameter a = 0.586–0.588 nm) and the coarse κ'' ((Fe,Mn)3AlCx) carbide (ordered L12-type, a = 0.387–0.389 nm) as [111]κ'' || [220]D03 and (011)κ'' || (001)D03. The figure provides direct diffraction evidence for the crystallographic registry between the ferritic matrix and intragranular or boundary κ'' precipitates, supporting the duplex microstructural model of the alloy system.

This figure comprises two line-graph subplots presenting the room-temperature mechanical properties of as-cast Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C alloy sheet castings, labeled alloys A through H (corresponding to 5–40 mass% Mn). Subplot (a) plots ultimate tensile strength (UTS, solid diamonds) and yield strength (YS, solid squares) in MPa versus alloy designation, while subplot (b) plots elongation (EL, solid diamonds) in percent and hardness (solid squares) in HRC versus alloy designation. Visually, both UTS and YS in (a) rise from alloy A to a maximum at alloy C (15 mass% Mn) and then progressively decline toward alloy H; the caption reports peak values of 887 MPa for UTS and 824 MPa for YS at this composition. Hardness in (b) follows a similar non-monotonic trend, reaching a maximum near alloy C (approximately HRC 43) before decreasing to about HRC 31 in alloy H. Conversely, elongation increases monotonically from roughly 16% in alloy A to about 29% in alloy H. These opposing trends demonstrate that increasing Mn content initially strengthens the duplex austenite-plus-ferrite microstructure via carbide precipitation and solid-solution effects, but beyond 15 mass% Mn the declining α/D03 phase fraction and associated coarse κ″ carbide distribution reduce strength and hardness while markedly improving ductility.

Figure 9 presents three scanning electron microscope (SEM) fractographs of as-cast Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C alloys: (a) Fe–10Al–15Mn–1.0C with a 60 μm scale bar, (b) Fe–10Al–25Mn–1.0C with a 4.0 μm scale bar, and (c) Fe–10Al–35Mn–1.0C with a 1 μm scale bar. Subfigure (a) shows relatively coarse fracture features associated with the duplex γ+α microstructure, whereas (b) and (c) reveal progressively finer fracture surface details at higher magnifications as the Mn content increases from 25 mass% to 35 mass%. The accompanying text reports that the (D03 + κ'') two-phase region plays an important role in the fracture mechanism. The observed evolution in fracture morphology across these three compositions correlates with the systematic decrease in α-ferrite phase fraction and the associated changes in mechanical properties, including ultimate tensile strength and elongation, supporting the conclusion that manganese content strongly influences the microstructural constituents and fracture behavior of these Fe–Al–Mn–C sheet castings.

This figure presents a panel of eight frequency response function (FRF) curves obtained from experimental modal analysis on as-cast Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C (x = 5–40 mass%) alloy sheet castings prepared by investment casting. Subfigures (a) through (h) correspond to Mn contents of 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35 and 40 mass%, respectively. Each subfigure plots the magnitude of the FRF (y-axis shown in dB, with units of mass s^-2 N^-1 according to the caption) versus frequency in Hz (x-axis, 0–1.6 kHz linear scale), revealing a dominant resonance peak that corresponds to the natural frequency of the cantilever-mounted specimen. The peak frequencies visible in the plots range from approximately 226 Hz to 242 Hz, and the maximum peak magnitudes (YMAX) vary between 17.0 dB and 29.7 dB across the composition series. As reported in the caption and associated text, these FRF measurements were analyzed by the half-power point method to extract damping ratios and Young’s moduli. The systematic variation in peak shape and natural frequency with Mn content demonstrates the evolution of dynamic mechanical response, supporting the conclusion that increasing Mn content progressively lowers the damping capacity while shifting the elastic modulus in the Fe–Al–Mn–C alloy system.

Fig. 11 is a line chart plotting the damping ratio versus manganese content for as-cast Fe–10Al–xMn–1.0C alloy sheet castings. The horizontal axis is labeled Manganese Content (mass%) and spans 5 to 40 mass%, while the vertical axis shows Damping Ratio from 0 to 0.07. Eight data points are plotted for nominal Mn contents of approximately 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, and 40 mass%, displaying a clear monotonic decrease from about 0.060 to 0.042 as Mn content increases. The caption and citation context state that these damping ratios were extracted from frequency response functions (FRF) obtained by experimental modal analysis using the half-power point method, and that finite element analysis of a cantilever beam was used to fit natural frequencies and determine Young’s modulus. This figure demonstrates that increasing Mn content progressively reduces the damping capacity of the present alloys, a relationship the authors report has not been previously observed in the Fe–Al–Mn–C alloy system.
